section.title: A Resolve to Walk Blamelessly
section.sectionRationale: A royal psalm reading like David's own inaugural pledge, this hymn moves from private integrity at home to public rejection of slander and pride to daily governance in the city of the LORD, a portrait of righteous kingship that only Christ, the true and greater Son of David, fulfills without failure.
section.thesis: Because he will sing of the LORD's loving devotion and justice, the king resolves to walk with integrity in his own house, to reject every worthless and deceitful thing near him, and to purge wickedness daily from the city of the LORD.
section.frameNote: Three movements: personal vows of praise and integrity at home, verses 1-4; the rejection of slander, pride, and deceit from his presence, verses 5-7; and the daily purging of the wicked from the city of the LORD, verse 8.

units.label: I will walk with integrity of heart
units.note: personal vows of praise and integrity

verses.text: A Psalm of David. I will sing of Your loving devotion and justice; to You, O LORD, I will sing praises.
I will ponder the way that is blameless, when will You come to me? I will walk in my house with {{tamim|integrity}} of heart.
I will set no {{beliyaal|worthless thing}} before my eyes. I hate the work of those who fall away; it shall not cling to me.
A perverse heart shall depart from me; I will know nothing of evil.

units.label: No one who practices deceit
units.note: the rejection of slander, pride, and deceit

verses.text: Whoever slanders his neighbor in secret, I will put to silence; the one with haughty eyes and a proud heart, I will not endure.
My eyes favor the faithful of the land, that they may dwell with me; he who walks in the way of integrity shall minister to me.
No one who practices deceit shall dwell in my house; no one who tells lies shall stand in my presence.

units.label: Every morning I will remove the wicked
units.note: the daily purging of the city

verses.text: Every morning I will remove all the wicked of the land, that I may cut off every evildoer from the city of the LORD.

tamim.gloss: Blameless, complete, having integrity. The king vows to walk in his own house with this wholeness of character, a standard only fully met by Christ, the truly blameless King, Hebrews 7:26.

beliyaal.gloss: Worthless, wicked, later used as a name for the devil himself, 2 Corinthians 6:15. The king resolves to set nothing of this worthless kind before his eyes, guarding what he allows into his household and heart.

basic.context: Reading like an inaugural pledge for a new king's household and court, this psalm moves from David's own vows of personal integrity, refusing worthless things and perverse hearts, to his public rejection of slanderers and the proud, to his resolve to purge wickedness from the city of the LORD every single morning.
basic.hermeneutics: David's vows describe an ideal of righteous kingship rooted in his own historical reign, yet Scripture is honest that David himself did not perfectly keep these resolves; the psalm therefore functions both as a genuine aspiration for every leader and as a pattern that points beyond David to a King who keeps it fully.
basic.originalLanguages: The king resolves to walk in tamim, blameless integrity, within his own house, and to set no beliyaal, worthless thing, a word later applied to the devil himself, before his eyes. Personal holiness and public governance are held together as one seamless resolve.
basic.typology: David's resolve to walk blamelessly in his house, surround himself with the faithful, and purge deceit from his court anticipates Israel's ideal King as pictured in Isaiah 11:3-5, one who judges with righteousness, fully realized only in Christ, the King who is truly without sin, Hebrews 7:26.
basic.christ: Where David's own reign fell short of this psalm's ideal, most notably in his sin against Uriah and Bathsheba, Christ fulfills it perfectly as the King who 'loved righteousness and hated wickedness,' Hebrews 1:9, and whose cleansing of the temple, John 2:13-17, embodies this psalm's refusal to allow deceit or corruption in God's own house.
basic.god: God is the standard by which the king measures his own integrity, His loving devotion and justice together are the subject of the king's opening song, and it is specifically 'the city of the LORD,' not merely the king's own city, from which evil must be purged.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 17:18-20 requires Israel's king to keep God's law daily so his heart would not be lifted up in pride, the same concern this psalm expresses. Proverbs 25:5 counsels removing the wicked from the king's presence so his throne is established, echoing this psalm's resolve. Isaiah 11:3-5 pictures the ideal Messianic King who judges with righteousness. Hebrews 1:9 declares that Christ loved righteousness and hated wickedness, fulfilling this psalm's vow perfectly.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's royal resolve toward personal integrity and public righteousness, a standard David himself could not keep without failure, runs forward to Christ, the true and greater King who loved righteousness and hated wickedness without exception.

context.literary: Structured as a sequence of first-person royal vows, moving outward from the king's own heart and household to his court and finally to the whole city, this psalm reads like a policy statement set to song, its resolve pattern echoed in 1 Timothy 3 and Titus 1's qualifications for church leaders.
context.historical: Bearing the superscription 'Of David,' this psalm likely dates from early in his reign, once he had moved from a wandering fugitive's life to the settled responsibilities of ruling from Jerusalem, matching the description of his justice and righteousness in 2 Samuel 8:15.
context.canonical: Functions in the Psalter as a bridge between the enthronement psalms celebrating the LORD's own kingship, Psalms 93-100, and the personal lament of Psalm 102, applying the theology of God's righteous reign to the practical conduct of a human king under Him.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To set forth, in the form of personal vows, the standard of integrity and righteous governance expected of Israel's king, beginning with private holiness at home and extending to the purging of wickedness from the whole community.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm records David's specific personal resolutions as king of Israel; it prescribes for every leader, and indeed every believer, the abiding pattern of guarding one's private integrity before pursuing any public standard of righteousness in others.

debates.question: Given that David himself later failed to live out these very vows, most notably with Bathsheba and Uriah, how should this psalm be read?

views.label: As a genuine, if imperfectly kept, aspiration meant to be read alongside David's failures as evidence of his ongoing need for grace
views.case: Scripture does not hide David's sin, so this psalm's ideals should be read honestly as goals David sincerely held yet did not flawlessly achieve, underscoring humanity's need for a greater King.

views.label: As an early, pre-fall statement of intent, later betrayed, showing how even sincere resolve cannot secure lasting righteousness apart from ongoing grace
views.case: The psalm's placement without any note of later failure suggests it captures a genuine moment of resolve; its later betrayal by David's own sin illustrates precisely why the psalm's ideal ultimately requires fulfillment beyond any human king.

debates.bearing: Either way, the psalm's very existence alongside David's later failure points forward to the need for a King who can keep every vow this psalm makes, fulfilled only in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The king vows to walk in tamim, blameless integrity, within his own house before ever addressing his court. He resolves to set no beliyaal, worthless thing, a word later personified as a name for the devil, before his eyes, guarding both his private life and his public household.

typology.type: The king's resolve to walk blamelessly and reject all worthless things, verses 2-4
typology.fulfillment: Hebrews 7:26 describes Christ as the High Priest who is 'holy, innocent, undefiled, separated from sinners,' the only King who fulfills this psalm's vow without failure.

typology.type: The purging of deceit and evildoers from the city of the LORD, verses 7-8
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' cleansing of the temple, John 2:13-17, embodies this psalm's refusal to allow corruption to remain in God's own house.

study.christ: Where David's own life shows that even a sincere king could not keep this psalm's vows without failure, Christ fulfills them perfectly. Hebrews 1:9 declares of the Son, 'You have loved righteousness and hated wickedness,' the very posture this psalm pledges, and Jesus' zeal in cleansing the temple, John 2:13-17, embodies this psalm's resolve that deceit and corruption should have no place in the house and city of the LORD.
study.god: God is the object of the king's song of loving devotion and justice, the standard against which the king measures his own integrity, and the true owner of the city from which wickedness must be purged, 'the city of the LORD,' not merely the king's own possession.

crossReferences.note: Requires Israel's king to keep God's law daily so that his heart would not be lifted up in pride, the same discipline this psalm pledges.
Counsels removing the wicked from the king's presence so that his throne may be established, echoing this psalm's resolve to surround himself with the faithful.
Pictures the ideal Messianic King who judges with righteousness and faithfulness, the standard this psalm sets for David's own reign.
Declares that Christ loved righteousness and hated wickedness, fulfilling this psalm's royal vow without the failure David himself experienced.

study.oneStory: This psalm's portrait of a king vowing personal integrity, rejecting deceit and pride, and purging wickedness from the city of the LORD, a standard David himself could not keep without failure, is fulfilled in Christ, the true and greater Son of David who loved righteousness and hated wickedness perfectly.

quiz.q: Where does the king first resolve to walk with integrity in verse 2?

options.text: Within his own house.
options.note: The king begins with private integrity at home before ever addressing his public court or city.

options.text: In the marketplace, before merchants and traders.
options.note: The psalm's first resolve is specifically about his own house, not the marketplace.

options.text: On the battlefield, before enemy armies.
options.note: The psalm's concern in verse 2 is domestic integrity, not military conduct.

options.text: In the royal court, before foreign ambassadors.
options.note: Court matters appear later in the psalm, but the first resolve named is specifically about his own house.

quiz.q: What does the king vow to set before his eyes in verse 3?

options.text: No worthless thing.
options.note: Verse 3 records the vow, 'I will set no worthless thing before my eyes.'

options.text: Only images of past military victories.
options.note: The vow is a rejection of worthless things generally, not a specific endorsement of war imagery.

options.text: Whatever pleases the royal court.
options.note: The vow is precisely the opposite, a deliberate refusal of whatever is worthless, regardless of court preference.

options.text: Only scrolls of the law, to the exclusion of all else.
options.note: While devotion to God's law fits the psalm's spirit, the specific vow named is the rejection of worthless things, not an exclusive focus on scrolls.

quiz.q: How does the king respond to slanderers, according to verse 5?

options.text: He will put to silence whoever slanders his neighbor in secret.
options.note: Verse 5 explicitly states this resolve against secret slander.

options.text: He will reward those who bring him gossip about others.
options.note: The psalm's resolve is precisely to silence slander, not reward it.

options.text: He will ignore slander entirely as beneath his notice.
options.note: The text states an active resolve to silence slander, not passive indifference.

options.text: He will require slanderers to make a public apology only.
options.note: The psalm states he will put slanderers to silence, a stronger and more active response than simply requiring an apology.

quiz.q: What kind of people does the king favor to dwell with him and minister to him, in verse 6?

options.text: The faithful of the land, those who walk in the way of integrity.
options.note: Verse 6 explicitly favors the faithful and those walking in integrity for his household and service.

options.text: The wealthiest citizens, regardless of character.
options.note: The stated criterion is faithfulness and integrity, not wealth.

options.text: Only members of his own extended family.
options.note: The criterion given is character, faithfulness and integrity, not bloodline.

options.text: Skilled diplomats known for clever, flattering speech.
options.note: The psalm favors integrity and faithfulness specifically, in contrast to mere cleverness or flattery.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament present Christ in relation to this psalm's royal ideal?

options.text: As the King who actually loved righteousness and hated wickedness, fulfilling the psalm's vow where David himself later failed.
options.note: Hebrews 1:9 applies this very language to Christ, and His sinless life fulfills the psalm's standard without David's later failure.

options.text: As someone entirely unrelated to royal ideals of integrity or governance.
options.note: Hebrews 1:9 and other texts directly connect Christ to exactly this royal standard of righteousness.

options.text: As a King who abolishes the very concern for integrity and holiness in leadership.
options.note: Christ does not abolish this concern; He fulfills and embodies it perfectly.

options.text: As a King whose righteousness is identical in degree to David's own, no greater and no less.
options.note: Christ's righteousness surpasses David's, since Christ, unlike David, never failed to love righteousness and hate wickedness.

questions: The king's resolve begins at home, 'I will walk in my house with integrity of heart,' before it ever reaches his public life. Where is the gap widest between how you act privately and how you present yourself publicly?
The psalm vows to set 'no worthless thing' before the eyes. What specific media, habits, or influences might you need to remove from what you regularly allow yourself to see or dwell on?
The king resolves to silence slander rather than tolerate it. Where have you allowed gossip or slander a place in your conversations that this psalm would call you to actively refuse?
David himself, who wrote these vows, later failed to keep them. How does knowing this shape the way you approach your own resolutions to grow in godliness, with humility rather than self-reliance?

The psalm ends with a resolve to purge wickedness 'every morning,' a daily discipline rather than a one-time decision. What daily practice might help you renew, rather than merely make once, your own commitment to integrity?

translationNotes.note: The question 'when will You come to me?' interrupts the king's vows with a plea for God's own presence and help, showing that his resolve for integrity depends on God's enabling, not merely human willpower.
The Hebrew term rendered 'worthless thing,' beliyaal, later becomes a proper name associated with the devil himself, 2 Corinthians 6:15, 'What harmony is there between Christ and Belial.'

'Every morning' reflects the ancient Near Eastern custom of kings holding court and rendering judgments at the city gate at the start of each day, a practice attested at sites such as Dan and Lachish.

meta.notes: The local scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error in this sandbox, so the BSB text was obtained via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com/bsb/psalms/101.htm, cross-checked against the section map's 8-verse bound. Footnote markers were stripped and the em dash in verse 2 of the source was converted to a comma. The superscription 'A Psalm of David.' was folded into verse 1 as printed by the source, keeping the verse count at 8. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) could be confirmed this session as preached specifically on Psalm 101, so status is 'none' rather than risk fabrication.
